file_name: WOMENSGOLFUNLIMITEDINC_03_29_2000-EX-10.13-ENDORSEMENT AGREEMENT - Intellectual Property Rights                 Confidentiality and Non-Use Obligations Agreement.txt

perturbation.original_text: 11.7. INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS, CONFIDENTIALITY AND NON-USE. The Professional acknowledges her obligations under the provisions of the Intellectual Property Rights Confidentiality and Non-Use Obligations Agreement attached hereto as "Exhibit A" and made a part hereof by this reference. The rights and obligations of the parties set forth in Exhibit A shall survive the termination or expiration of this endorsement agreement, regardless of cause or circumstances of the termination or expiration.
perturbation.changed_text: 11.7. INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS, CONFIDENTIALITY AND NON-USE. The Professional acknowledges her obligations related to Intellectual Property Rights, Confidentiality and Non-Use, as outlined in subsequent sections of this agreement. The rights and obligations of the parties related to these matters shall survive the termination or expiration of this endorsement agreement, regardless of cause or circumstances of the termination or expiration.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the explicit reference to 'Exhibit A,' which details the specific obligations regarding intellectual property, confidentiality, and non-use. This creates an in-text contradiction because later sections, such as section (f), still define and assign Intellectual Property rights as if Exhibit A were still part of the agreement, creating uncertainty about the actual scope and nature of these obligations. It is assumed by later mentions of Intellectual Property, that the parties are refering to exhibit A, but the exhibit itself has been removed.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (f) Recipient further acknowledges and agrees that all new discoveries, inventions, improvements, processes, formulae, designs, drawings, training materials, original works of authorship, photos, video tapes, electronic images, documentation, trademarks and copyrights (the "Intellectual Property"), that may be developed, conceived, or made by Recipient, alone or jointly with others during her work for the Company, shall be the exclusive property of the Company and shall be deemed a work for hire. Recipient hereby assigns and agrees to assign all Recipient's rights in any Intellectual Property to the Company. Recipient hereby grants to the Company power of attorney for the purpose of assigning all Recipient's rights in Intellectual Property to the Company for the purposes of filings, registrations and other formalities deemed necessary by the Company to prosecute, protect, perfect or exploit its ownership and interests in Intellectual Property. Recipient further agrees to execute, acknowledge and deliver any documentation, instruments, specifications or disclosures necessary to assign, prosecute, protect, perfect or exploit the Company ownership of Intellectual Property.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit reference to Exhibit A, which the original text used to define Intellectual Property rights, confidentiality, and non-use obligations. The contradicted text, section (f), explicitly defines and assigns 'Intellectual Property' rights to the company, implying the existence and terms of Exhibit A. Without Exhibit A, section (f)'s definition and assignment lack a clear basis, creating a contradiction. The omission of Exhibit A undermines the foundation upon which the later assignment of Intellectual Property rights rests.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement by and between SQUARE TWO GOLF INC., a New Jersey corporation (the "Company") and KATHY WHITWORTH, an individual residing at 302 La Mancha Court, Santa Fe, New Mexico 87501 (the "Recipient"), is part of the Endorsement Agreement of the parties. In consideration of and as an inducement for the Company entering into said Endorsement Agreement with Recipient: (a) Recipient acknowledges and agrees that communications for the purpose of proposing to work for or working for the Company have in the past or will entail the disclosure, observation and display to Recipient of information and materials of the Company that are proprietary, confidential and trade secret, which include, but are not limited to, golf equipment marketing plans, research, development and designs, computer software, screens, user interfaces, systems designs and documentation, processes, methods, fees, charges, know-how and any result from the work performed by Recipient or the Company, new discoveries, Intellectual Property (as defined below) and improvements to the Company's products made for or on behalf of the Company (all of which, singly and collectively, "Information").
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement by and between SQUARE TWO GOLF INC., a New Jersey corporation (the "Company") and KATHY WHITWORTH, an individual residing at 302 La Mancha Court, Santa Fe, New Mexico 87501 (the "Recipient"), is part of the Endorsement Agreement of the parties. In consideration of and as an inducement for the Company entering into said Endorsement Agreement with Recipient: (a) Recipient acknowledges and agrees that communications for the purpose of proposing to work for or working for the Company may entail the disclosure of Company information. The Recipient is expected to maintain a reasonable level of confidentiality with respect to disclosed information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a detailed description of what constitutes "Information" including specific examples of trade secrets and proprietary data. The modified text removes these specific examples and replaces them with a vague expectation of "reasonable level of confidentiality." This creates a contradiction because section (g) refers to 'valuable know-how, proprietary, confidential and trade secret Information that has been procured or developed by the Company at great expense' without clear definition, which were removed in the perturbation location. This leaves ambiguity about the scope of the information that is protected, contradicting the specificity assumed in section (g).
perturbation.contradicted_text: (g) Recipient acknowledges and agrees that the Company possesses valuable know-how, proprietary, confidential and trade secret Information that has been procured or developed by the Company at great expense and that its unauthorized disclosure would result in substantial damages to the Company that may not be adequately compensated by monetary relief. Accordingly, Recipient hereby consents to the jurisdiction of the Federal and County Courts in Essex County, New Jersey and agrees that the Company may seek temporary restraining orders against it or other extraordinary relief necessary to protect the Information.
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the definition of 'Information' by removing specific examples and replacing them with a vague expectation of 'reasonable level of confidentiality.' This directly contradicts section (g), which refers to 'valuable know-how, proprietary, confidential and trade secret Information' without a clear definition, creating ambiguity about the scope of protection. The original text provided a specific and detailed understanding of what constituted "Information". Because of the Omission, the contract now implies 'Information' is less than it actually is, and less protected, causing the explicit contradiction

perturbation.original_text: (d) All Information, including any copies thereof, in any media, in the possession or control of Recipient and Information embodied or included in any software or data files loaded or stored on computers in the possession or control of Recipient, its agents or employees, shall be removed and returned to the Company upon demand, but no later than the completion of work for the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: (d) Upon termination of the agreement, the Recipient is encouraged to return all Company-related materials.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates the return of all 'Information,' including copies in any media, upon demand or completion of work. The modified clause replaces this with a suggestion that the Recipient is 'encouraged' to return 'Company-related materials.' This creates a contradiction because Section (e) states that the recipient agrees she will not copy the Information in whole or in part or use all or any part of the Information to reverse engineer, duplicate the function, sequence or organization of the Information for any purpose without the prior written permission of the Company. Section (e) refers to the information section (d) is supposed to return, however due to the perturbation, section d is now a suggestion. Since it is now a suggestion, it contradicts the Information section (e) is refering to, as it no longer mandatory to give it back, but instead a suggestion.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (e) Recipient agrees that she will not copy the Information in whole or in part or use all or any part of the Information to reverse engineer, duplicate the function, sequence or organization of the Information for any purpose without the prior written permission of the Company.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated the return of all 'Information,' including copies, upon demand or completion of work, ensuring the company retained control over its proprietary information. The changed text replaces this with a suggestion that the Recipient is 'encouraged' to return 'Company-related materials.' Section (e) prohibits copying or reverse engineering of the Information and assumes the recipient doesn't retain it after termination. The change introduces a contradiction because Section (e) refers to 'the Information', but due to the change, section (d) now suggests returning the information instead of mandating it. This means that it is not mandatory to give it back, which contradicts the intention and requirement in Section (e) which assumes the information will be returned.
